proposals.fieldLabel: Organic traffic decline section — replace generalities with quantified structural shift
proposals.currentValue: organic traffic decline
proposals.proposedValue: The Great Decoupling: B2B discovery queries down 70–80%, AI Overviews present on 13.14% of queries (up from 6.49%), zero-click rates at 80–83% when present. Rising search volumes mask collapsing exit clicks.
proposals.reason: Current phrasing lacks specificity and impact. Quantified metrics (70–80% B2B query decline, 13.14% AI Overview prevalence, 80–83% zero-click rates) ground the structural shift in measurable data and reinforce the 'Great Decoupling' framing: search volume growth decoupled from click-through traffic.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 1 — Replace "organic traffic decline" generalities with quantified structural shift: B2B discovery queries down 70–80%, AI Overviews on 13.14% of queries (up from 6.49%), zero-click rates at 80–83% when present. Frame as "The Great Decoupling" (rising search volumes, collapsing exit clicks).

proposals.fieldLabel: SEO/AEO/GEO definitions and framework structure
proposals.currentValue: SEO AEO GEO definitions, three-discipline framework, what each discipline measures
proposals.proposedValue: AEO (Answer Engine Optimization) = AI-powered features within Google Search (AI Overviews, featured snippets, instant answers); GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) = standalone LLM platforms (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude). Define fundamentally different ranking factors: AEO focuses on citation eligibility and snippet inclusion within Google's AI layer; GEO focuses on citation ranking, source credibility, and retrieval preference in external LLM systems. Platform-specific optimization factors differ significantly from universal SEO ranking signals.
proposals.reason: Current framework conflates AI features within Google's search ecosystem with standalone LLM platforms, obscuring distinct optimization strategies, ranking mechanisms, and measurement approaches. The proposed distinction clarifies that AEO and GEO require different content strategies—AEO requires snippet optimization and Google-specific credibility signals, while GEO requires citation-worthy content and source authority optimization across independent AI platforms.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 2 — Separate vague "SEO/AEO/GEO" into distinct strategies: AEO = AI-powered features within Google (Overviews, snippets); GEO = standalone LLM platforms (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude). Define fundamentally different ranking factors per platform (citation vs. ranking, platform-specific vs. universal).

proposals.fieldLabel: RAG pipeline explanation depth
proposals.currentValue: RAG pipeline overview covering crawling, chunking, vector embeddings, retrieval, content freshness in AI index
proposals.proposedValue: RAG pipeline with hybrid retrieval mechanics: keyword + semantic matching, reranking algorithms, multi-stage filters. Clarify freshness is publication-date-based, not update-date. Include stat: 24% of ChatGPT responses cite zero online content. Replace content freshness myth with continuous republishing/update signaling strategies.
proposals.reason: Current overview is oversimplified. Hybrid retrieval mechanics and multi-stage filtering are critical to understanding how AI systems actually rank and cite sources. The 24% zero-citation stat challenges the 'freshness premium' assumption and redirects focus to republishing signals over passive updates.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 3 — Expand RAG pipeline from oversimplified overview to hybrid retrieval mechanics (keyword + semantic), reranking, multi-stage filters. Clarify freshness is publication-date-based, not update-date; 24% of ChatGPT responses cite zero online content. Replace "content freshness" myth with continuous republishing/update signaling strategies.

proposals.reason: Empirical citation data and platform-specific correlation metrics provide actionable guidance superior to generic format recommendations. Negative backlink correlation contradicts traditional SEO assumptions and requires explicit removal to avoid misguidance.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 6 — Replace generic format recommendations with data-driven content matrix by platform: listicles = 25.37% of citations; word count correlates +0.191 for Perplexity but +0.047 for ChatGPT; semantic completeness (r=0.89) critical for Google AI Overviews. Remove backlink correlation assumptions (shows negative across all platforms).

proposals.fieldLabel: Module 7 core thesis
proposals.currentValue: The Earned Media Inversion
proposals.proposedValue: Owned Media Primary: Balance earned media as authority signal while recognizing that 86% of AI citations come from brand-managed sources
proposals.reason: Research data shows brand-owned content drives majority of AI citations, contradicting emphasis on third-party PR as primary citation driver. Reframing maintains earned media's role in authority while accurately reflecting citation patterns.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 7 — Reframe thesis from "Earned Media Inversion" to "Owned Media Primary": 86% of AI citations come from brand-managed sources, contradicting emphasis on third-party PR. Balance earned media as authority signal, not primary citation driver.

proposals.reason: Google AI Overviews are now permanent (2026), fundamentally breaking last-click attribution. Brands must measure dark funnel visibility where users receive answers from AI summaries without clicking through, requiring new tracking methods for LLM discovery across platforms.

proposals.fieldLabel: Crocodile Mouth insight and framing
proposals.currentValue: AI is answering questions directly, so users never need to click through.
proposals.proposedValue: AI is answering questions directly in zero-click environments. Success now means earning citations in AI-generated summaries, not maintaining traditional rankings—citations drive discovery even when users never visit your site.
proposals.reason: 2026 data shows 58.5–59.7% zero-click rates and 70–80% organic traffic loss for B2B discovery queries. Reframing shifts the module's core message from defensive (maintaining rankings) to offensive (earning AI citations as the primary business objective in a zero-click world).
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 1 — Reframe zero-click search from trend to structural business problem; shift from "maintain rankings" to "earn AI citations despite zero-click environment" per 2026 data showing 58.5–59.7% zero-click rate and 70–80% organic traffic loss for B2B discovery queries

proposals.fieldLabel: RAG pipeline steps — add RAG Quality Engineering subsection
proposals.currentValue: { id: 6, label: 'Semantic matching', detail: 'Chunks most similar to the query\'s meaning are selected' }
proposals.proposedValue: RAG Quality Engineering subsection after step 6 covering: (1) Reranking strategies — cross-encoder models that re-score retrieved chunks by relevance; (2) Semantic chunk boundaries — intelligent segmentation using sentence/paragraph markers vs. fixed token windows; (3) Multi-query expansion — reformulating user queries to improve retrieval recall; (4) Reciprocal rank fusion — combining results from multiple retrieval methods; (5) Freshness signals in vector databases — weighting recent embeddings higher in similarity search. Include LangChain and LlamaIndex orchestration examples showing how these techniques integrate into production RAG pipelines.
proposals.reason: RAG quality engineering is critical to citation probability but currently absent from Module 3. This addition bridges the gap between high-level RAG mechanics and implementable AEO strategies, directly improving content's retrievability and ranking within AI-generated responses.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 3 — Add "RAG Quality Engineering" subsection covering reranking strategies, semantic chunk boundaries, multi-query expansion, reciprocal rank fusion, and freshness signals in vector databases; include LangChain/LlamaIndex orchestration examples

proposals.fieldLabel: Citation ranking factors - replace generic guidance with platform-specific data
proposals.currentValue: Statistics boost visibility up to 40% — including statistics in content is one of the highest-impact GEO signals.
proposals.proposedValue: Platform-specific citation ranking factors vary significantly: ChatGPT prioritizes domain rating (0.161 correlation) and Wikipedia presence; Perplexity favors word count (0.191 correlation) and recency; Google AI Overviews show strongest correlation with semantic completeness (r=0.89). Traditional SEO metrics (backlinks, domain authority) now negatively correlate with AI citations across all platforms.
proposals.reason: Action item requires replacing generic citation factors with empirical platform-specific ranking tables and data showing that traditional SEO metrics are inversely correlated with AI citation performance in 2026.

proposals.fieldLabel: Module 4 Dark Funnel Framing and Content Expansion
proposals.proposedValue: Add new lesson section (l4_4) titled 'The Dark Funnel: How AI is Changing B2B Buying' that reframes untracked AI citations as a fundamental shift in how B2B buyers research solutions rather than simply a tracking problem. Include: (1) definition of dark funnel as the research conversations happening in AI tools like ChatGPT where vendors can't see prospect activity; (2) how to estimate dark funnel's impact on your pipeline using simple attribution models that account for invisible touchpoints; (3) 'dark funnel scores' as early warning signals that measure how often your brand appears in AI conversations, how frequently it's mentioned, and how relevant those mentions are; (4) real example showing how dark funnel scores can predict which prospects are about to enter your sales pipeline 30-60 days before they contact you; (5) practical methods for measuring AI-driven research including brand monitoring tools, tracking ChatGPT referral patterns, and connecting AI activity to your CRM data.
proposals.reason: Current Module 4 focuses on SEO foundations and passage-level optimization but lacks substantive coverage of the dark funnel as a strategic measurement and buying system shift. The action item expands this to position dark funnel as a core B2B intelligence signal, introducing probabilistic attribution and dark funnel scores as leading indicators—critical for B2B teams operating in AI-mediated research environments where traditional attribution fails.

proposals.fieldLabel: Earned media inversion concept with platform-specific weighting
proposals.currentValue: AI systems show a systematic bias toward earned media over brand-owned content. If a third-party publication says Aria is great, that citation is worth far more than Aria saying it itself.
proposals.proposedValue: AI systems show a systematic bias toward earned media over brand-owned content, though weighting varies by platform type. Wikipedia and Reddit citations carry higher peer-validation weight for generalist queries, while analyst reports and industry publications dominate authority-driven citations. If a third-party publication says Aria is great, that citation is worth far more than Aria saying it itself—yet 86% of citations still originate from brand-owned sites, reflecting the challenge of earned media capture at scale.
proposals.reason: Research context shows AI systems apply different credibility weights to source types. Adding platform-specific guidance helps practitioners understand which third-party channels matter most for their category, while the 86% statistic reveals the paradox: most citations still come from brand sites despite AI's preference for earned media, highlighting the gap between AI preference and citation reality.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 7 — Add platform-specific weighting guidance showing how different AI systems prioritize source types (Wikipedia/Reddit for peer validation vs. analyst reports for authority) and that 86% of citations still originate from brand-owned sites
proposals.discardReason: The detail suggested comes later in this lesson. You should review the content of the lesson before making suggestions on individual pieces of it.

proposals.fieldLabel: Organic traffic decline narrative and insight
proposals.currentValue: Across 42 B2B websites tracked in 2025-2026, impressions grew 31% year-over-year. But clicks fell 18%. The gap between them keeps widening, like a crocodile's mouth opening. More visibility, fewer visitors.
proposals.proposedValue: Across 42 B2B websites tracked in 2025-2026, impressions grew 31% year-over-year. But clicks fell 18%. The gap between them keeps widening, like a crocodile's mouth opening. This represents a distribution shift, not elimination: mid-market sites (ranked 100–10,000) experience the largest relative declines, while traffic is concentrating among citation winners in AI summaries. Traffic is being redistributed, not erased.
proposals.reason: EnFuse research shows organic traffic decline is concentrated in mid-market sites and driven by redistribution to citation winners rather than overall elimination. This reframe aligns with the research context finding that 'SEO remains alive and significant, though user behaviour, SERP features, and interface changes are reshaping how traffic is distributed and monetised rather than erasing the channel altogether.'
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 1 — Reframe organic traffic decline narrative from "50%+ decline by 2028" to emphasize distribution shift and citation winner concentration; note EnFuse finding that mid-market sites (ranked 100–10,000) experience largest relative declines while traffic redistribution, not elimination, is occurring

proposals.fieldLabel: Freshness decay curve with platform-specific refresh windows
proposals.proposedValue: Content freshness decay follows a measurable curve: content updated within 7 days sees the highest citation rate, a 30-day window shows 3.2x higher citation rate compared to stale content, and a 3-month cliff triggers dramatic visibility drop. Platform-specific refresh requirements vary: Perplexity requires 2–3 day refresh cycles for optimal citation visibility, while traditional SEO and Google AI Overviews operate on monthly to quarterly refresh cycles.
proposals.reason: The current lesson covers citation drift and freshness importance but lacks quantified decay metrics and platform-specific refresh requirements. Adding these data points enables practitioners to prioritize refresh cadences by platform and understand the specific citation impact of content age.

proposals.fieldLabel: LLM Query Type mapping section with content architecture patterns

proposedValue.heading: AEO Query Architecture: Four Core Intent Patterns
proposedValue.body: AEO is not a content format exercise—it's an architecture discipline. Understanding how LLMs interpret and structure queries reveals how to position your content for citation. Four core query patterns dominate AI search: What is X? (definitional), How to X? (instructional), Best X? (evaluative), and Compare X vs Y? (comparative). Each pattern triggers different retrieval strategies and citation weights. By mapping your content architecture to these intent patterns, you transform AEO from formatting rules into a structural advantage.
keyPoints: What is X? — Definitional queries favor concise, entity-rich answers with schema markup and knowledge graph integration
How to X? — Instructional queries prioritize step-based structure, answer-first formatting, and machine-readable process flows
Best X? — Evaluative queries reward comparison frameworks, criterion-based scoring, and third-party validation signals
Compare X vs Y? — Comparative queries cite sources that provide structured side-by-side analysis, tables, and differentiators
Content architecture discipline: Design information hierarchy around LLM retrieval logic, not human reading patterns

proposals.reason: Module 2 currently defines AEO as a citation layer but lacks practical guidance on how content architecture aligns with LLM query interpretation. Adding this section positions AEO as a strategic discipline (architecture-first) rather than a tactical one (format-first), directly addressing the action item and providing immediate structural guidance for content teams.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 2 — Add LLM Query Type mapping section covering four core intent patterns ("What is X?", "How to X?", "Best X?", "Compare X vs Y") and how to structure content architecture around these patterns, positioning AEO as architecture discipline not format exercise

proposals.fieldLabel: Three-tier dark funnel measurement framework lesson

proposedValue.heading: Measuring the Dark Funnel: Three-Tier Framework
proposedValue.body: AI citations often happen without clicks or trackable referrals. A three-tier measurement framework helps marketers quantify dark funnel impact without engineering support. Tier 1 captures proxy signals, Tier 2 aggregates behavioral patterns, and Tier 3 models attribution.
keyPoints: Tier 1 (Proxy Metrics): Track AI referral traffic growth, share of voice in AI summaries, and brand mentions across AI platforms — establishes baseline visibility without requiring API access
Tier 2 (Signal Aggregation): Combine account-level behavioral data and peer network mentions to detect citation patterns and identify which content formats drive AI citations
Tier 3 (Attribution Modeling): Use mix modeling and direct traffic attribution to correlate AI visibility with downstream conversions and account engagement
Practitioner-level implementation: All three tiers can be set up with existing analytics tools (Google Analytics, brand monitoring platforms) without custom engineering

proposals.reason: Action item requests adding a practitioner-friendly three-tier dark funnel measurement framework to Module 4. Current Module 4 content focuses on SEO foundations but lacks guidance on measuring untracked AI citations and attribution—critical for marketers in the AI search era.
proposals.comment: This is all well and good, but today we don't have the ability to address this in our platform so it feels like minutia that we can't make actionable today.
proposals.rawActionItem: [ADD] Module 4 — Add three-tier dark funnel measurement framework: Tier 1 (proxy metrics: AI referral traffic growth, share of voice in summaries, brand mentions), Tier 2 (signal aggregation: account-level behavioral data, peer network mentions), Tier 3 (attribution modeling) with practitioner-level KPIs marketers can implement without engineering support
proposals.discardReason: This is all well and good, but today we don't have the ability to address this in our platform so it feels like minutia that we can't make actionable today.

proposals.fieldLabel: Opening narrative framing in lesson 1_1
proposals.currentValue: Aria's marketing team just pulled the monthly analytics report. Organic traffic is down 18%. But revenue is up 35%. That shouldn't be possible, unless the way customers find Aria has fundamentally changed.
proposals.proposedValue: Aria's marketing team just pulled the monthly analytics report. Organic traffic is down 18%. But revenue is up 35%. That shouldn't be possible, unless the way customers find Aria has fundamentally changed. Search visibility itself is shifting—some traffic comes through traditional organic clicks, some through AI citations, some through direct discovery. The mix varies, but the underlying reality is the same: visibility now depends on being found across multiple surfaces, not just Google's blue links.
proposals.reason: Reframes narrative from collapse to bifurcation, acknowledging that top-tier domains maintain growth while mid-market faces structural realignment rather than decline. This avoids alienating mid-market B2B teams whose pipeline still depends 40–50% on traditional organic.
proposals.comment: Don't get into enterprise vs. midmarket here, it confuses the ask
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 1 — Reframe narrative from "organic search decline" to "search bifurcation"; emphasize that top-tier domains maintain traffic growth while mid-market must compete for AI citation share; acknowledge structural realignment rather than collapse to avoid alienating mid-market B2B teams deriving 40–50% of pipeline from traditional organic

proposals.fieldLabel: AI platform scale metrics and revenue impact
proposals.proposedValue: Google AI Overviews now reach 2 billion monthly users (up from 1.5 billion). ChatGPT has 700 million weekly active users. Critically: AI traffic converts 4.4× higher than traditional organic search. This means even a 2–3% AI traffic share could represent 25%+ of pipeline revenue by end of 2026.
proposals.reason: Action item requests refresh of AI platform scale metrics with new user figures and revenue conversion data to reflect current market reality and business impact.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 1 — Refresh AI platform scale metrics: Google AI Overviews now reach 2B monthly users (up from 1.5B); ChatGPT 700M weekly active users; add that AI traffic converts 4.4× higher than traditional organic, making 2–3% AI traffic share potentially worth 25%+ of pipeline revenue by end of 2026

proposals.fieldLabel: New lesson on multi-platform citation fragmentation
proposals.proposedValue: Multi-platform citation fragmentation: AEO success requires optimization across multiple AI platforms, not just Google. LinkedIn is now the #1 most-cited domain for B2B and professional queries (surge from outside top 20 in November 2025), while only 13.7% citation overlap exists between ChatGPT and AI Overviews. This means ranking well on Google does not guarantee visibility on other AI platforms. Successful AEO strategy must account for platform-specific citation patterns and optimize content for each discipline independently.
proposals.reason: The course currently treats AEO as a unified discipline but does not address the critical reality that different AI platforms cite different sources. The dramatic rise of LinkedIn citations and low cross-platform overlap require explicit strategic guidance.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 2 — Add multi-platform citation fragmentation guidance; note that LinkedIn is now #1 most-cited domain for B2B/professional queries (surge from outside top 20 in Nov 2025) and that only 13.7% citation overlap exists between ChatGPT and AI Overviews, meaning ranking well on Google doesn't guarantee visibility on other platforms

proposals.fieldLabel: Platform-specific and content-type freshness cycles in lesson l5_2
proposals.proposedValue: Freshness requirements vary significantly by platform and content type: Blog/evergreen content requires 2–4 week refresh cycles; journalism/news content shows sharp citation decay after 7 days and should be updated 7–14 days; professional B2B guides typically need monthly or quarterly updates with less age penalty. Perplexity requires 2–3 day update cycles for maximum visibility, while AI Overviews tolerate longer refresh cycles.
proposals.reason: The current lesson l5_2 discusses freshness importance and citation drift but lacks platform-specific and content-type guidance. Adding granular refresh cycle recommendations will help content strategists optimize citation performance across different AI platforms and content formats.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 5 — Expand freshness premium guidance to distinguish by platform and content type: Blog/evergreen content 2–4 week refresh cycles; journalism/news 7–14 days (sharp citation decay after 7 days); professional B2B guides monthly or quarterly (less age penalty); note Perplexity requires 2–3 day updates while AI Overviews tolerate longer cycles

proposals.fieldLabel: B2B GenAI deal outcomes statistic
proposals.proposedValue: 90%+ of B2B buyers using GenAI for deals $1M+ report positive outcomes, validating that AI-assisted research drives higher-quality purchasing decisions.
proposals.reason: Adds critical B2B buyer behavior data showing GenAI adoption correlates with successful deal outcomes, strengthening the module's argument that the search revolution improves conversion quality.
proposals.rawActionItem: [UPDATE] Module 1 — Add that 90%+ of B2B buyers using GenAI for deals $1M+ report positive outcomes, and that 75% of enterprise B2B companies are increasing budget for influencer relations because buying groups rely on external experts discovered through AI search, not brand-owned content alone
